title: The Impact of Bank Credit on Labor Reallocation and Aggregate Industry Productivity
abstract: We provide evidence that the deregulation of U.S. state banking markets leads to a significant increase in the relative employment and capital growth of local firms with higher productivity, and that this effect is concentrated among young firms. Using financial data for a broad range of firms, our analysis suggests that this effect is driven by a shift in the composition of local bank credit supply toward more productive firms. We estimate that this effect translates into economically important gains in aggregate industry productivity and that changes in the allocation of labor play a central role in driving these gains.

article.introduction:
A growing body of evidence suggeststhat improvements in financial markets can significantly contribute to economic growth and that increases in aggregate productivity play a central role in this process.1Despite the importance of this idea, we still have limited direct evidence on the specific economic mechanisms through which finance affects growth and productivity. In this paper, we study the role of local U.S. banking markets in shifting the allocation of labor and capital within local industries toward firms with higher productivity. Intuitively, such reallocation changes the composition of an industry and plays an important role in increasing the industry's aggregate productivity as it allows a given level of aggregate resources to generate more output.2Banks can potentially be important in shaping this resource reallocation because of their role in determining which firms are financed. Therefore, improvements in banking markets can lead to a more positive link between firm productivity and bank credit. We examine this idea and the importance of labor in this process. While labor is a central factor used in production, limited attention has been paid to the role of labor reallocation in explaining the effects of finance on aggregate productivity.
We study the importance of bank lending for both labor and capital reallocation in the context of major U.S. state banking deregulations, which reduced constraints on banks’ ability to expand across geographic markets and represent historically important changes in U.S. banking markets. An advantage of analyzing these reforms is that they capture focused changes in the degree of bank competition in a local market. Broad patterns around these reforms indicate that the previous reallocation effects appear to be important in driving higher economic growth during these episodes (Jayaratne and Strahan (1996,1998)). As banking markets become more competitive, banks—new or existing—might improve the screening and monitoring of borrowers. This can happen, for example, because of an improved selection mechanism among surviving banks or because a given bank faces stronger incentives for performance. Improvements in the screening of borrowers should make it easier for banks to detect higher productivity firms. Additionally, improvements in the monitoring of firms should make it easier for borrowers to pledge their future income. Firms with higher productivity can arguably benefit more from these improvements because they have more income to pledge.
However, in theory, the sign and magnitude of this effect are unclear. Increased bank entry may simply lead to a reduction in market power and interest rates, increasing the availability of credit for all firms. Reductions in bank market power can also harm relationship formation (Petersen and Rajan (1995)) and lead banks to lend less to all small businesses. In addition, new (larger) banks entering a market might not have a comparative advantage in small business lending and hence may be worse in monitoring and screening these firms.
Empirically analyzing the importance of the above reallocation effects has been difficult due to data and identification challenges. Such analysis requires microlevel data on small firms connecting their real decisions and sources of external finance to their productivity and age. We analyze small firms by using firm- and plant-level data from the U.S. Census Bureau. Two recent studies by Kerr and Nanda (2009) and Krishnan, Nandi, and Puri (2015) also use these data to analyze the effects of deregulation. However, these studies do not examine the reallocation of production from less productive to more productive firms, nor the impact on labor versus capital, and they do not study the bank financing patterns of these small firms.3We also quantify the magnitudes of the industry productivity gains implied by our results.4
We implement our analysis using microlevel data from the U.S. Census Bureau on a broad sample of small U.S. manufacturing firms. These data allow us to relate firms’ real decisions and financial policies to measures of their multifactor productivity estimated using plant-level data. We argue that the above reallocation effects should be most important for the youngest firms. Because of older firms’ established lending relationships, new firms are more likely to borrow from new banks entering a market. If new banks change the composition of local credit supply, then this effect should concentrate among the youngest firms. Differences in the screening and monitoring of banks should also matter more for young firms, which have a limited track record, and these firms should depend more on external funds to finance their higher growth.5
We start by analyzing how the relative growth of higher productivity firms within a local industry changes after deregulation in their state. We estimate this effect separately for different firm age groups and examine its differential importance for young existing firms. Our main results employ a triple-difference framework, where we compare changes in relative growth across states experiencing deregulation at different points in time, and then contrast this effect for different age groups. We find that deregulation is associated with an economically large increase in the relative growth of more productive young firms. Moreover, this relative growth increase is important for both labor and capital, and it is not present for older firms. We further find that this increase in growth takes place right after deregulation and is not associated with preexisting growth trends.
We next investigate the role of changes in the composition of credit supply in driving the above growth effect. First, we directly examine the importance of such changes in the composition of credit supply. As before, we separately examine different age groups and focus on differential patterns for young firms. To analyze the role of bank credit supply, as opposed to credit demand, we analyze how firms borrow across different sources of debt financing. Our data allow us to distinguish between debt from commercial banks (bank debt) and debt from other sources (nonbank debt), and deregulation only covered commercial banks. Consistent with previous research (Petersen and Rajan (1994)), we find that young and old small firms rely significantly on both bank and nonbank debt. We argue that changes in credit demand should affect firm demand for both sources of debt financing. In contrast, increases in bank credit supply should lead to an increase in the share of total debt coming from banks in a given firm-year. We therefore estimate our results using this share of bank debt as an outcome.6We find that deregulation is associated with a significant increase in high-productivity firms’ relative bank debt share. This effect is economically large for the young firms that drive the previous growth results while it is limited for old firms for which the growth effects are not present. Moreover, these effects are associated with modest changes in the average bank debt share of young or old firms.
We also examine the composition of borrowing across bank debt and nonbank debt. We find economically large increases in the relative importance of bank debt for high-productivity firms. In contrast, we find no evidence of increases in the relative importance of nonbank debt for these firms. This suggests that the increase in the relative use of bank debt by high-productivity firms is not explained by credit demand. Additionally, we find that improvements in local economic conditions that are unrelated to local banking deregulation do not predict the patterns in the composition of firms’ debt financing that we document. These financial results provide direct evidence that our growth effects are associated with a significant shift in the composition of bank credit supply toward more productive firms. Both of these effects are important only among young firms, and their estimated magnitudes are consistent with the view that a shift in credit supply drives the growth effects.
We conduct additional tests to further mitigate the possibility that deregulation explains the growth effects through alternative mechanisms that differentially affect the growth of young, high-productivity firms. First, because deregulation is positively associated with local economic growth (Jayaratne and Strahan (1996)), we examine the effect of exogenous shocks positively affecting local economic conditions. We test whether such shocks lead to an increase in the relative growth of high-productivity firms that is differentially important for young firms. We examine shocks to overall state economic conditions as well as shocks to subsets of local industries. More specifically, we consider the role of local demand effects by examining shocks to other local industries, which are significant customers. Our results indicate that these local demand effects do not explain our growth results.7We also analyze potential effects through local input markets and local labor markets. Across a range of falsification tests, we provide direct evidence that leading alternative mechanisms cannot explain our growth results.
In a last set of analyses, we examine the implications of our results for aggregate industry productivity. In order to guide these tests, we use a simple monopolistic competition framework employed in recent research connecting resource misallocation to industry productivity (e.g., Hsieh and Klenow (2009,2014)). We focus on the implications of a change in the composition of credit supply in such a setting. We illustrate how these implications depend on how increased access to credit affects firms’ cost of using capital or labor. While models of finance and misallocation typically assume that financial frictions only increase firms’ cost of using capital, recent empirical research on finance and labor implies that these frictions should also increase firms’ cost of using labor.8Theoretically, these labor effects can be more important than capital effects in increasing industry productivity because of the larger share of labor in production. Accordingly, we first examine whether deregulation leads to significant convergence in the marginal revenue products of young firms with high versus low productivity. Our framework illustrates that this convergence should be present for labor only if labor misallocation effects are important. We find that deregulation is associated with significant convergence in the labor marginal revenue product gap of young firms with different productivity and that this pattern is similar for capital.
We also use our framework to quantify the magnitude of the implied gains in industry productivity and consider the role of labor in determining them. Here we account for the fact that our results concentrate on a subset of young firms and consider the implications of deregulation across all states for the aggregate productivity of a given industry. Our results imply gains between 1.1% and 2.1% of industry value added over a few years. This effect is equivalent to a 3.3% to 6.2% increase in the output of the small young firms driving our results. We also find that labor misallocation plays a central role in these gains because of the larger share of labor in production.
Overall, our paper makes three main contributions. First, we provide direct evidence that the deregulation of U.S. state banking markets leads to a significant reallocation of resources toward young, more productive firms, and that such reallocation results in economically important gains in aggregate industry productivity. Second, we provide evidence that these effects are driven by a shift in the allocation of bank credit toward high-productivity firms. This finding highlights a specific mechanism through which competition in local banking markets can matter for aggregate productivity. Finally, we provide evidence that the allocation of labor—not only of capital—can play a central role in determining the quantitative implications of finance for aggregate productivity.9
In addition to previous research on U.S. banking deregulation, our analysis complements studies on the cross-country relation between financial development and the allocation of capital across industries (Wurgler (2000)) and on changes in this allocation within U.S. industries over time (Lee, Shin, and Stulz (2016)). Our analysis also complements studies that examine how country-level reforms in banking or financial markets affect the allocation of resources (Bertrand, Schoar, and Thesmar (2007), and Larrain and Stumpner (2017)). Our study is the first to examine how microlevel changes in the composition of bank lending affect both capital and labor reallocation and industry productivity.
The remainder of this paper is organized as follows. In SectionIwe discuss the institutional details on banking deregulation. SectionIIpresents the data sources and summary statistics. Results on labor and capital reallocation are discussed in SectionIII. SectionIVcontains findings on the bank credit channel, and SectionVanalyzes the impact of these findings on aggregate productivity. Finally, in SectionVI, we present our concluding remarks.
article.I. State Banking Deregulation:
Prior to the 1970s, most U.S. states had long-standing restrictions on banks’ ability to operate within and across state borders. Given that small U.S. firms largely relied on geographically close banks as a source of external financing until the early 1990s (Petersen and Rajan (2002)), these restrictions created local banking monopolies. Between the early 1970s and early 1990s, states relaxed these restrictions in a staggered way. Following previous research on U.S. state banking deregulation, we focus on two main types of restrictions imposed by states. First, states imposed restrictions on intrastate branching. These restrictions included restrictions on the ability of multibank holding companies to convert branches of acquired subsidiary banks into branches of a single bank, as well as restrictions on banks’ ability to open new branches. As in Jaraytane and Strahan (1996), we use the date of intrastate deregulation as the date when a state permits branching through mergers and acquisitions.
Second, the Douglas amendment to the Bank Holding Act of 1956 prevented a bank holding company from acquiring banks in another state unless that state explicitly permitted such acquisitions by statute. No state allowed such acquisitions until the late 1970s. States then entered reciprocal regional or national arrangements that allowed their banks to be acquired by banks in any other state in the arrangement. Except for Hawaii, all states had entered into such agreements by 1993. These episodes of interstate deregulation culminated with the passage of the 1994 Riegle-Neal Interstate Banking and Branching Efficiency Act, which codified these state-level changes at the national level. We begin our sample in 1977, when our data are first available, and we end our sample in 1993, based on the above timeline. We follow Amel (1993) and Kroszner and Strahan (1999, hereafter KS) in identifying the dates of interstate and intrastate deregulation.
We construct a state-level banking deregulation index (Dereg) that combines these two types of events. Specifically, we defineDeregas the sum ofIntrastate_DeregandInterstate_Dereg, whereIntrastate_DeregandInterstate_Deregare indicators equal to one if the state has passed intrastate and interstate banking deregulation by the given year, respectively. We list the dates for each deregulation event in our sample period by state in Table IA.1. Figure1plots the average (across states)Interstate_Dereg,Intrastate_Dereg, andDeregfor each year in our sample period. The significant deregulation wave that took place around the mid-1980s is clearly visible. KS argue that technological changes such as the development of more sophisticated credit scoring models made it more difficult for local interest groups to protect local banking monopolies.
Previous research documents that state banking deregulation is associated with local economic growth and suggests that changes in the composition of bank credit supply appears to be the channel driving such an effect (Jayaratne and Strahan (1996, hereafter JS)). JS argue that deregulation might trigger a shift in the composition of bank lending toward higher quality borrowers, which increases the share of total investment coming from firms with better growth opportunities (composition effect). They conjecture that such changes in the allocation of bank credit might come from improvements in the screening and monitoring of the average local bank. Their evidence suggests that deregulation in a given state is not associated with significant increases in aggregate loan growth in that state. At the same time, deregulation is associated with reductions in the aggregate importance of nonperforming loans or charge-offs for banks in deregulated states. Jayaratne and Strahan (1998) find that most of the state-level decline in loan losses can be explained by the fact that better performing banks increase their market share in a state after deregulation. This evidence is consistent with the idea that deregulation improves the composition of local bank lending by creating a stronger selection mechanism for local banks.
However, these studies do not provide direct microlevel evidence on the importance of the bank credit composition effect. Providing such evidence is important for at least two broad reasons. First, banking deregulation could explain the previous broad patterns through multiple economic channels, which could have different implications for when and how local banking competition matters. Improvements in the efficiency of local banks could lower their costs and translate into lower interest rates for all firms, reducing implicit barriers to entry and facilitating the entry of new nonfinancial firms that displace less efficient incumbents.10Second, evidence on the channel through which deregulation matters can also help better establish its importance.11
We examine how deregulation affects the relative growth of high-productivity firms. Our focus on differences in firm productivity within an industry is motivated by previous empirical and theoretical work on industry productivity and resource misallocation.12Analyzing the previous composition effect requires firm-level data linking differences in firm productivity to their real and financial decisions. Moreover, since local banks should matter most for small firms, it is important to have microlevel data covering such firms.
article.II. Data and Summary Statistics:
Our main data sources are the Longitudinal Business Database (LBD), the Census of Manufacturers (CM), and the Quarterly Financial Reports (QFR), all of which come from the U.S. Census Bureau. The CM provides information on the sales and inputs used by all manufacturing firms every five years (i.e., Census years). Our analysis tracks changes in the relative growth of more productive firms around deregulation events. Because many deregulation events happen between Census years, higher frequency data are needed to precisely analyze how a given firm changes around such events. We therefore combine the CM with the LBD. The LBD provides annual employment information for every private establishment from 1976 onward. The underlying data are sourced from U.S. tax records and Census Bureau surveys. We use the LBD to annually track within-industry differences in firm employment growth and link to the CM to relate these differences to firm productivity. We construct the main sample used in our growth results by matching firms in the LBD and the last available CM.
We focus on firms whose operations are concentrated in a single state. State banking deregulation should matter most for firms with strong geographic exposure to banks in the deregulated state. We also focus our analysis on firms that operate in a single industry, as comparing the productivity of different firms operating in an industry is challenging when firms also operate in other industries. After imposing these two restrictions, almost all firms in our data are single-plant firms, which is the focus of our analysis. Overall, across all manufacturing firms in the LBD or the CM over our sample period, we find that 76% to 77% of establishments belong to single-plant firms. When we consider the total number of firms operating in a given industry and year (i.e., firm-industry-year observations), we find that 91% of firms are single-plant firms.
Our analysis examines differential patterns among young firms, which are plausibly more exposed to the effects that we want to isolate. We measure a firm's age from the first year in which the firm appears with positive employment in the data. We construct age groups based on the difference between the current year and this first year of operations. Our main age groups areYoung1andYoung2, which include firms present for the first time in the previous CM and present for the first time in the CM before the previous one, respectively;Oldincludes the remaining firms.13The former groups capture firms of age 1 to 5 and 6 to 10 in the previous CM, respectively. In our analysis, we focus on composition effects among these young firms with a single plant. While these firms represent a subset of the overall sample of firms in an industry, we explicitly take this into account when examining the implications of our results for aggregate industry productivity in SectionV.14
Panel A of TableIIpresents summary statistics for the overall sample and for the subsamples of young firms. As described in Section I.A, our sample covers the period between 1977 and 1993. All variables are described in AppendixA. The average firm in our overall sample is small, with 22.3 workers and 1.4 million in sales ($1987). In some of our additional tests on the real effects of deregulation, we restrict our analysis to Census years. This comes from examining outcomes not covered in the LBD (e.g., capital stock growth) or a focus on longer term effects of deregulation (e.g., the analysis in SectionV). In these tests, we use only the CM to construct similar samples of single-plant firms in different age groups.
Our analysis of changes in firms’ financial policies is based on the QFR. The QFR collects quarterly balance sheet and income statement data on large, medium, and small manufacturing corporations. These data are available at the firm level during Census years, and we construct annual firm-level values for the variables in these years using averages for each firm. We construct our sample for this analysis by matching the QFR (with annual values) and the CM in Census years. As before, we only include single-plant firms. We also note that the QFR uses a random sample of firms, which differ from the population of firms in the CM and LBD. We construct sample weights based on firm size and age that replicate the importance of size and age groups in our previous sample described in Panel A. By estimating results using these weights, we estimate effects that are representative of the same sample considered in our analysis of real effects. This sample includes firms in 1977, 1982, 1987, and 1992.
Panel B of TableIdescribes the main variables used in this analysis. We report summary statistics with the sample weights used in the analysis. We focus on variables that capture the importance of bank debt and nonbank debt financing. Companies separately report their debt from commercial banks (bank debt) and other sources in the QFR. We measure the importance of bank debt using the ratio of bank debt to total debt (Bank Debt Share). We also separately measure the ratios of bank debt and nonbank debt to total assets, which we labelBank Debt RatioandNonbank Debt Ratio, respectively. This distinction is important in our analysis because only commercial banks are directly affected by banking deregulation. Between 40% and 50% of firms’ debt comes from nonbank sources for both young and old firms. This shows that firms significantly rely on both bank and nonbank debt. Additionally, the combination of bank and nonbank debt represents approximately 30% of firms’ assets, with this ratio significantly larger for young firms, for which this combination represents approximately 40% of firms’ total assets. This suggests that the young single-plant firms in our analysis rely significantly on debt financing. These values do not include trade credit and are similar to those described in Petersen and Rajan (1994) based on the National Survey of Small Business Finances (NSSBF) for the late 1980s.15
As discussed in Section I.B, an important variable in our analysis is firm total factor productivity (TFP). We include measures of firm TFP using data from the CM. Following previous research in finance examining productivity, we employ a simple strategy to estimate plant-level TFP. In particular, we estimate a Cobb-Douglas production function with separate parameters for each industry-year (three-digit SIC code). We estimate these parameters using ordinary least squares (OLS) with year fixed effects and data from the previous five years.16As a robustness check, we report our main credit composition and labor reallocation results using alternative approaches for estimating TFP in Tables IA.2 and IA.3, respectively. Since we focus on single-plant firms, our measures of firm productivity are equivalent to plant productivity.
article.III. Banking Deregulation and Labor and:
We start our analysis by examining how the relative growth of high-productivity firms changes after deregulation. We analyze this effect separately for different age groups and focus on the differential effect for young firms. By examining immediate changes in young firms’ growth around deregulation and comparing such changes to those for young firms in states that did not deregulate in the given year, we can better isolate the effect of these events from other changes affecting industries in a state. This higher frequency analysis is important to compare a firm's experience before and after deregulation.
We estimate equation1separately for the age groupsYoung1,Young2, andOldand examine the differential value of β for young firms. In each age group, the estimation of β indicates how the sensitivity of firms’ growth to their productivity (TFP) in a local industry relates to state banking deregulation. The increase in firm growth predicted byTFPcaptures the sensitivity of this growth to a given gap in firm productivity, which is measured within an industry-state-year because of the fixed effects.
Intuitively, one can think of this estimation as involving two steps. First, we estimate the previous sensitivity within each industry-state-year. Second, we estimate how deregulation affects this relationship using a difference-in-differences specification. We implement these two steps together in a single regression.17When we contrast this estimated effect for different age groups, we are analyzing a triple difference, which tells us the differential change in the above sensitivity for young firms. This last interaction allows us to contrast the effect of deregulation on different groups of firms in the affected states and control for broad changes around deregulation in the sensitivity of industries in the affected states.
We initially focus on employment growth because this is an important outcome that can be measured at an annual frequency for a broad range of firms. Previous research provides direct evidence that bank credit availability affects the employment of small and medium firms (e.g., Chodorow-Reich (2014)), so changes in the composition of credit should translate into shifts in relative employment growth.18TableIIreports these employment growth results. All reported coefficients are scaled to capture changes in the relative growth of firms in the top versus bottom quartiles of an industry-state. More precisely, we multiply the estimated coefficients by the average TFP gap between these two groups. We start by analyzing the average effect of deregulation across age groups.
Column (1) of Panel A reports the results, which are based on the estimation of equation1with the addition of the controlsYoung1,Young2, as well as their interactions with the two deregulation variables. The results show that deregulation is associated with a significant increase on average in the relative growth of high-productivity firms. This result implies that deregulation (i.e., a change inDeregbetween zero and two) is associated with a 1.8% increase in the relative employment growth of firms in the top TFP quartile.
Column (2) of Panel A examines the differential effect forYoung1. In this specification, all independent variables (including controls) and fixed effects in equation1are included with and without their interaction withYoung1—the only variables not interacted withYoung1areAge1andAge2. Column (3) examines these differential effects separately forYoung1andYoung2. This last test is based on a specification where the independent variables and fixed effects are separately interacted withYoung1andYoung2. The results show that the previous effects are differentially stronger for, and driven by, the youngest firms: An economically and statistically significant increase in the relative growth of more productive firms obtains for the youngest firms, while the effect for older firms is economically small and statistically insignificant in column (1) and is negative in column (2). In particular, the results imply that, in the subsample of young firms (Young1), deregulation is associated with a differential increase of 3.0% to 4.2% in the relative employment growth of the top TFP quartile. Recall from TableIthat the average employment growth of these young firms in the sample is 5.7%.
We next consider the possible role of firm age and size in explaining these results. If differences in firm TFP capture these characteristics, then the above results would reflect changes in the link between firm growth and age or size. In the context of age, we note that we are comparing firms within an age group and year. For our main group of interest (Young1), within-group-year differences in age are limited to five years. We also include continuous measures of firm age based on the LBD (Age1andAge2) as controls.19As an additional robustness check on the role of age differences, we estimate our previous tests without these last controls. Columns (1) and (2) of TableII, Panel B, report the estimated effects. The coefficients on the interaction withYoung1are similar in the results with and without these controls. We also consider an alternative definition ofYoung1using the LBD. Here,Young1is an indicator equal to one if the firm is between one and five years old using the LBD. Note that this measure can be defined only from 1982 on (see SectionII). Column (3) of Panel B reports the results based on this variable, which includes younger firms than the previousYoung1. The results are economically stronger using this alternative specification.
We next consider the role of size. In contrast with age, a firm's size should be endogenously affected by its productivity as more productive firms should find it more attractive to expand their business. Previous models and evidence on resource allocation within industries emphasize this idea and the existence of a strong positive link between size and productivity within an industry. Under these frameworks, productivity is an exogenously given firm characteristic and conditioning on firms’ choice of size while examining a link with productivity can bias our estimates. Our main tests therefore use a broad control for firm size. In particular,Largeis an indicator equal to one if the firm is above the median size (employment) within the industry-age group-year. Columns (1) and (2) of Panel C (TableII) show how the results change as we drop this size control. We next estimate the effects using narrower group sizes as controls. In particular, in columns (3) and (4) of Panel C we include indicators for all size quintiles within an industry-age group-year, constructed in an analogous way as forLarge. Across the two sets of results, we find that our documented effects remain similar and indeed become economically stronger as we add size controls. Together with the previous age analysis, these results indicate that the results in TableIIare not capturing a change in the link between employment growth and size or age within local industries.
We next check whether our previous employment growth effects are associated with changes in the relative growth of firms’ capital stock. If the above results are driven by changes in the composition of bank credit, it would be natural to expect them to be associated with capital effects. One challenge in implementing this analysis is that we observe firms’ capital stock growth for a broad range of small firms only in Census years. This is especially relevant because we focus on young firms—in a sample with only Census years, firms inYoung1are present in the data for the first time and one cannot contrast their growth before and after deregulation. Thus, in TableIII, we estimate the same specification used in TableII(columns (2) and (3) of Panel A) but exclude firm fixed effects.
TableIIIshows that our above effects are associated with an important increase in the relative capital growth of high-productivity firms in the sample of young firms. Deregulation is associated with a 0.8% increase in the relative capital growth of the top TFP quartile for young firms.
We also examine the dynamics of the above growth effects. We focus on employment growth because we can measure this outcome each year. We first consider whether the previous changes in relative growth after deregulation are associated with pre-existing trends. These results are based on specifications that add more variables to those in TableII(column (3) of Panel A). More specifically, we addDereg(−1to−3)orDereg(−1to−6), which we construct in an analogous way asDereg, using the sum of indicators that now equal one in the three or six years prior to a deregulation event, and we include the same set of interactions as forDereg. TableIVreports the results.
The results show that our effects are not associated with pre-existing positive trends in the relative growth of young firms with high productivity—such growth changes only after deregulation. To shed additional light on our main effects, we repeat this analysis using narrower windows of time. In particular, we split our sample into three subperiods (1977 to 1982, 1983 to 1988, and 1989 to 1992) and estimate our main tests separately for each subperiod. We then calculate the average effect across these periods. Notice that the average effect can be estimated only by contrasting the period right after deregulation with the period right before deregulation. The results, reported in Table IA.4, indicate that our previous findings are robust to narrowing the window of time and focusing on growth immediately after a deregulation event.
article.IV. How Does Banking Deregulation Affect:
We now examine how changes in the composition of bank lending affect our differential growth effects for young, productive firms. In particular, we study whether there is a shift in the composition of bank credit supply toward more productive firms. We also examine whether the magnitude of these results is consistent with the view that they drive the real effects above. We then conduct a series of robustness tests that consider the potential role of alternative mechanisms in explaining the relative growth effects.
In this section, we examine whether differential higher growth for young, productive firms after deregulation is associated with changes in the composition of bank credit supply. In doing so, we examine different age groups separately to determine whether changes in the composition of bank credit are more important for young firms. We link a firm's bank debt to its productivity for a broad range of firms, and weight firms using sample weights to match our previous sample of single-plant firms.
A challenge in isolating credit supply effects is the fact that deregulation may also affect firms’ demand for bank debt. We address this issue by examining how firms borrow across different sources of debt financing. Our data allow us to measure both bank debt (from commercial banks) and nonbank debt (from other sources). Only commercial banks are directly affected by state banking deregulation.20We argue that changes in credit demand should affect firms’ demand for both bank and nonbank debt, while an increase in bank credit supply should lead to an increase in the share of total debt coming from banks (i.e., bank debt share) for a given firm-year. Thus, as a first approach to isolate bank credit supply effects, we examine whether deregulation is associated with a more positive link between firms’ productivity and their bank debt share. This basic approach of using the share of bank debt to isolate shifts in bank credit supply is also employed in Kashyap, Stein, and Wilcox (1993) and Becker and Ivashina (2014).
As before, the estimation of β within an age group can be interpreted as a difference-in-differences analysis where the outcome variable is the relation between firms’ bank debt share and productivity. We examine whether the difference in this relation between initially deregulated states and other states is more positive prior to the deregulation wave. Our data cover the years 1977, 1982, 1987, and 1992, and the deregulation wave takes place in the middle of this period (see Figure1). Notice that, relative to our previous results, this estimation includes one additional level of comparison (bank debt versus nonbank debt).
Panel A of TableVreports the results for different age groups. To facilitate comparison with previous tables, we multiply the estimated effects by the gap in TFP between the top and bottom quartiles of an industry-state. The results show that deregulation is associated with an economically and statistically more positive link between firms’ bank debt share and productivity for young firms. This effect is also positive for old firms (age 11+) but is statistically significant only for young firms, and the economic magnitude of this effect for old firms is approximately eight times smaller than that for the youngest firms (age 1–5). The magnitude for young firms is also important in absolute terms. The interactionIDeregs×Pretcaptures a gap inDeregof approximately 1.0.
The effect for young firms (age 1–10) implies that deregulation (i.e., a change inDeregfrom zero to two) leads to a 0.41 increase in the relative bank debt share of top TFP firms. Note that this is a relative effect: In the absence of significant changes in the average value of firms’ bank debt share (see below), this estimate implies effects with the opposite sign and half of the magnitude for top and bottom TFP firms, and the mean value of firms’ bank debt share is around 0.55 for these same young firms (TableI).
We shed further light on these differences across age groups by estimating the differential value of the above effects for young firms. Panel B of TableVreports these differential values. We find that they are statistically significant and economically similar to the previous estimates for young firms. This similarity is expected given the small (relative) magnitude of the effect for the old firm group.
Figure2illustrates the dynamics of these effects, where the effects are estimated separately for each Census year (1977, 1982, 1987, and 1992). Figure1suggests that the deregulation wave becomes strong between 1982 and 1987. Figure2shows that the convergence effects occur mostly during this period and mostly among young firms. These observations further support the view that the financial patterns above capture banking deregulation.
Panel C of TableVreports the results using this alternative specification. Since the previous interactionIDeregs×Pretcaptures a gap inDeregof approximately 1.0, the magnitude of these results is directly comparable to those in Panel A. The effects remain qualitatively and quantitatively similar to those previously estimated. Panel D of TableVexamines whether these relative changes in the importance of bank debt for more productive firms are associated with significant changes in the average importance of bank debt. Here, we rely on the same interactionIDeregs×Pretused in Panel A but we now analyze the average changes inBankDShare, rather than the relative effects for high-productivity firms. The results show that the average changes in the share of bank debt are economically limited and statistically insignificant. These results suggest that deregulation mostly affects the composition of credit in each of these age groups, consistent with the evidence on banking deregulation discussed in Section I.B.
One concern with the above results is that deregulation could potentially affect firms’ relative demand for bank debt versus nonbank debt. We examine this possibility by separately analyzing changes in these two sources of debt financing. It is difficult to imagine that high-productivity firms would only increase their demand for bank debt in response to deregulation. Therefore, if the results are driven by increases in credit demand, rather than credit supply, for these firms, they should plausibly be associated with increases in both bank and nonbank debt. TableVIreports results using the same specifications as in TableVwith bothBank Debt RatioandNonBank Debt Ratioas the outcome variables. The results show that the effects among young firms are associated with an economically and statistically significant increase in the relative importance of bank debt (level) for high-productivity firms but a statistically insignificant and economically much smaller decrease in the importance of nonbank debt—consistent with changes in credit supply, not credit demand, driving the results. These patterns are again robust across specifications and do not hold for old firms.22
We also address the concern that it might be credit demand rather than credit supply that is responsible for our results with an additional falsification test. We examine whether economic shocks that affect a state can lead to the same patterns on the link between firms’ bank debt share and productivity that we document above. Specifically, we estimate specifications analogous to those in TableVexcept that we replaceIDeregwithShock, which captures state-level economic shocks during our sample, and we replacePrewithPost=1 − Pre. TableVIIreports the results. We first defineShockas the predicted shock to economic conditions in a state between 1977 and 1993. Constructed by combining the initial (employment) composition of industries in each state with the realized national-level (employment) growth of different industries over the same period, this measure captures exogenous shocks to state-level conditions.23We also defineShockas the growth in the state-level employment between 1977 and 1992, and control for the effect of deregulation. This analysis captures the effect of broad changes in state economic conditions not associated with banking deregulation. Using both approaches, we find that improvements in state economic conditions are not associated with the above patterns on firms’ bank debt share.
We next consider whether the magnitude of the financial results for young firms is consistent with the view that they drive the documented growth effects. The results for young firms in TableVI, Panel A imply a 14% increase in the relative bank debt ratio of the top TFP quartile. This increase in relative bank debt is equivalent to 14% of firms’ total assets after a 1.0 change inDeregover a period of approximately five years. Moreover, the employment growth effects for young firms in TableIIimply a 7.0% to 10.0% increase in relative employment over five years. These magnitudes suggest that the financial results can plausibly explain our previous growth results.
Our previous analysis provides direct evidence that the reallocation results are associated with a significant shift in the composition of bank credit supply toward young, more productive firms. We now analyze in greater detail whether other economic mechanisms could also explain our reallocation results. At a broad level, firms could be affected by deregulation indirectly through changes in local economic conditions, in addition to directly through changes in bank lending. For instance, deregulation might affect the entry of new local firms, as well as the decisions of local consumers or other local firms. This could then change the economic conditions an existing firm faces after deregulation. Another possibility is that deregulation might increase the contestability of local product markets for nonfinancial firms. This could then increase the incentives of incumbents to maximize their value, and in turn increase the alignment of firms’ decisions with their productivity. Alternatively, new firm entry or the expansion of existing firms could drive up prices for local inputs or labor. If less productive firms are more exposed to such local price increases, this could trigger a reallocation of resources toward more productive firms.
To explain our reallocation results, these alternative mechanisms would need to generate an increase in the relative growth of more productive firms that is present only among young firms. Some of these mechanisms, such as an increase in product market contestability, should naturally affect older incumbent firms. In the case of other mechanisms such as local demand effects, it is unclear whether changes in these conditions would differentially affect more productive firms. However, one might be concerned that older firms are larger and are less sensitive to local economic conditions—if some changes in local economic conditions increase the relative growth of high-productivity firms, the lower sensitivity of older firms could lead these effects to be relevant only for young firms. In contrast to this view, previous research in our setting provides direct evidence that existing firms—both young and old—are significantly responsive to local economic conditions (Adelino, Ma, and Robinson (2017)). Indeed, this research finds comparable sensitivities to local economic conditions across these two groups. We also note that our analysis focuses on single-plant firms and that the older firms in our sample are mostly small firms, with average employment equal to 29 workers (see TableI).24
Nonetheless, we address this concern in two ways. First, we build on our previous financial policy results. The alternative channels above predict that the increase in the relative growth of high-productivity firms should be associated with an increase in the relative credit demand of these firms. Under the alternative mechanisms, the increase in the relative growth of high-productivity firms is not driven by greater access to bank credit at the firm level. In contrast to the view that these channels drive our results, however, the results in Section IV.A suggest that deregulation is not associated with an increase in the relative credit demand of young firms with high productivity.
Second, we employ additional falsification tests that directly examine whether leading alternative mechanisms can generate the patterns in our growth results. More specifically, we examine whether positive shocks to local economic conditions can generate an increase in the relative growth of high-productivity firms that is differentially more important for young firms. Recall that deregulation is positively associated with local economic growth (Jayaratne and Strahan (1996)). We consider both broad shocks to state economic conditions as well as narrower shocks that focus on specific alternative mechanisms such as an increase in the local demand for an industry's products.
TableVIIIreports the results. These results are based on the estimation of a specification analogous to that analyzed in Panel A of TableII(columns (2) and (3)), which replacesDeregwithShock. The results include the same set of controls as in TableII, which are now defined using different definitions forShock. We describe the basic idea here; more details are provided in AppendixA. We first defineShockusing the two basic approaches employed in TableVIIto capture shifts in state-wide economic conditions. In Panel A,Shockis the predicted shock to state economic growth between 1977 and the current year. This shock is constructed by combining the initial (employment) composition of industries in each state with the realized national-level (employment) growth of different industries over the period (1977 to current year). The predicted shock is the weighted average of national-level shocks to industries based on initial weights. This measure captures exogenous shifts to state economic conditions triggered by national-level changes to its industries.
In Panel B,Shockis the growth in state-level employment between 1977 and the current year. This specification, which includesDeregand all of its interactions in TableIIas additional controls, provides evidence on shifts in state-level economic conditions that are not associated with state banking deregulation. As in our previous tests, we scale the estimated effects by multiplying them by the TFP gap between the top and bottom quartiles of an industry-state. To better capture the magnitude of the effects, we also multiply these estimates by the standard deviation ofShockin the sample being analyzed.25The results are presented in TableVIII. We find that positive changes to state-wide economic conditions do not lead to our growth results above. The magnitude of the estimated effects across all specifications is limited when compared to those in TableII. Moreover, in the cases in which the effects are economically stronger or statistically significant, they all have the opposite sign of our growth results.
After considering the role of broad changes in state-level economic conditions, we focus on specific alternative channels. In these tests,Shockis a predicted shock to the growth of a subset of other related industries in the same state between 1977 and the current year. The shock to this subset of connected industries is predicted analogously to the approach used in Panel A (using national-level shocks). Intuitively, positive shocks to local connected industries occur when these industries are growing faster at the national level. We follow Ellison, Glaeser, and Kerr (2010) in defining connected industries, which are defined at the national level and described in greater detail in AppendixA.26
In Panel C we examine whether positive shocks to local demand conditions predict the patterns in our growth results. This analysis is motivated by the possibility that deregulation can lead to an increase in the local demand that industries face, which leads in turn to the growth patterns above. For each industryi, we use input-output tables to classify industryjas a buyer if it purchases a sufficiently large share of the sales from industryi. This share is calculated using all customers, including final customers. We then capture demand shocks to an industry-state by predicting shocks to its local buyers (in the same state). The results show that these positive shocks to local demand are statistically insignificant and do not explain our results.
Panels D and E consider changes in local input and labor markets, respectively. These analyses address the previously discussed possibilities that positive shocks to local demand for industries’ inputs or labor could lead to the growth results that we document above. In Panel D, we use input-output tables to classify industryjas a supplier for industryiif an important share of the inputs used by industryicomes from industryj. This share is calculated using all suppliers. Positive shocks to the local input markets of an industry-state are then defined as shocks to its suppliers in the same state. In Panel E, for each industryi, we classify industryjas a common labor industry if both industryiandjuse workers in similar types of occupation. Similar to the previous analyses, positive shocks to the local labor markets of an industry-state are predicted shocks to common labor industries in the same state.
The results in Panels D and E show that positive shocks to input and labor markets are insignificant and do not generate the growth results above. The economic magnitude of these effects is limited when compared to those reported in TableII. While it is challenging to completely rule out the possibility of alternative mechanisms driving our results, our evidence is consistent with the view that the growth effects we document are explained by the impact of deregulation on the composition of credit supply among young firms.
article.V. Implications for Industry Productivit:
In this section, we examine the implications of the reallocation effects above for industry productivity. To guide our analysis, we build on a simple model of monopolistic competition with heterogeneous firms used in prior research connecting resource misallocation to industry productivity (Hsieh and Klenow (2009,2014)). We consider the role of the composition of bank credit supply in this framework.
We first use this framework to motivate simple tests of the hypothesis that our previous results lead to industry productivity gains. We solve for an expression that captures local misallocation losses by different groups of firms by industry and state, following our empirical analysis above. We then use this expression to quantify the implied magnitude of the gains from changes to bank credit supply resulting from bank deregulation.
This representative firm sells its output (industry output) in a perfectly competitive market at the pricePjt. These assumptions allow us to determine the demand for the differentiated products in the industry and lead to a constant elasticity of demand for these products.
This setting allows us to express industry output asQjt=TFPjt(Kjt)αj(Ljt)βj(Mjt)γj,whereTFPjtis industry productivity,Kjt≡∑i=1NjtKijtis the industry's capital, andLjtandMjtare the industry's labor and materials, respectively. Industry productivity changes when the industry produces more output with the same aggregate inputs. This will happen if individual firms become more productive or if the same aggregate industry inputs are used by more efficient firms. The allocation of resources is defined in broad terms and captures the distribution of factor shares across firms.27Conditional on a set of firms and their productivity distribution, industry productivity is maximized when resources are reallocated across firms to equalize marginal revenue products.28
We simplify our analysis by assuming that(Ait,MRPKit,MRPLit)are jointly log-normally distributed in an industry-year. Our analysis focuses on the intensive-margin allocation of resources and examines how changes in the composition of bank credit affect industry productivity conditional on the distribution of existing firms. Given this intensive-margin focus, the number of firmsNjtand the joint distribution of their characteristics(Ait,1+τKit,1+τLit,s(i,t),g(i,t))in the industry are exogenous to a firm.
In this setting, the contribution of the allocation of resources to industry productivity can be summarized by measures of the dispersion oflog(MRPLit)andlog(MRPKit)within the industry. Motivated by our empirical analysis, we examine the implications of the potential misallocation of resources across firms with different productivity within a group. Intuitively, the decrease in aggregate industry productivity induced by this within-group misallocation is captured by the dispersion oflog(MRPLit)andlog(MRPKit)within a group. Gaps in marginal revenue products within a group capture differences in distortions since all firms in a group face the same factor prices. Using a simple linear projection, we decompose firms’ distortions within groupgaslog(1+τFit)=bFg+aFg×log(Ait)+εFitg, whereCov(εFitg,log(Ait)|g,t)=0. The parameteraFgmeasures the incremental marginal product or distortion (in logs) predicted by a given increase in firm productivity within the group.
In the absence of this link between distortions and firm productivity, there are no (industry productivity) gains from reallocating resources across firms of different productivity within a group. The existence of this link is important in misallocation models and consistent with previous empirical evidence (Bartelsman, Haltiwanger, and Scarpetta (2013)). Building on this research, we assume thataKgandaLgare positive and find direct support for this assumption in our data.
We examine how a more positive link between credit supply and firm productivity (credit composition effect) affects the misallocation losses. We hold the distribution of firm productivity constant in this exercise. As in models of finance and misallocation, we assume that financial frictions affect the cost of using capital, which needs to be financed up front. The effect of the composition of credit on capital is captured by a reduction inaKg.
In contrast to previous models of finance and misallocation, but consistent with recent empirical research on finance and labor, we argue that financing frictions can also affect firms’ marginal cost of using labor. First, financial distress may affect the cost of using labor. In this case, greater access to credit can reduce the financial distress costs associated with labor. For example, because firing and training costs can limit firms’ ability to adjust their labor downward after a negative shock and wages tend to be sticky, labor can create operating leverage. As firms increase their labor, operating leverage becomes more significant, which increases the costs of financial distress.31Alternatively, because financial distress can expose workers to significant costs, firms may need to compensate workers up front for taking on such risks, increasing firms’ cost of using labor (Titman (1984), Berk, Stanton, and Zechner (2010)).32Similarly, if firing or losing workers during financial distress is costly for firms, the risk of such distress can increase firms’ up-front cost of using labor.
Second, a labor financing effect may arise from a need to finance labor expansions. Firms will need financing to expand labor if there is a timing delay between payments to workers and the cash flows generated by the use of labor—a net working capital channel recently studied theoretically by Jermann and Quadrini (2012) and empirically by Paravisini et al. (2015). Firms may also need financing to cover up-front hiring and training costs. Taken together, the above mechanisms suggest that financial distortions can affect firms’ marginal cost of using labor. We model labor distortions in an analogous way as capital distortions and allow the composition of credit to reduceaLg.
We assume that this credit composition effect occurs simultaneously for all groups (young and old) in a region with the magnitudes ranging across groups. We allow industry output and factor prices to change together with these credit effects. This incorporates potential shifts in demand and input markets that firms face after the credit effects. Under this framework, a shift in the allocation of credit always reduces local misallocation losses. This shift leads firm productivity to become less positively correlated with distortions and reduces the dispersion in marginal products within the group. Moreover, the number of regions affected by the credit composition effects and changes in industry output or input prices do not affect this analysis. Conditional on the basic parameters, changes in the correlation between distortions and firm productivity are sufficient to determine the previous drop in the dispersion in marginal products. Additionally, one can evaluate the importance of this effect by examining changes in the marginal revenue product gap predicted by differences in firm productivity within a group.
This framework also allows one to formally analyze whether labor might be a “sideshow.” We consider labor a sideshow if increases in credit supply reduce firms’ capital distortions but do not change their labor distortions. In this case, the local composition of credit will affect industry productivity only by changing the distribution ofMRPK(capital misallocation effect), with changes in the distribution ofMRPLhaving no role in explaining changes in local misallocation (labor misallocation effect). Note that, even if credit does not affect firms’ cost of using labor, the credit composition effects above will still be associated with labor reallocation toward more productive firms. This is due to the fact that capital and labor are complements and a relative expansion of capital by more productive firms also leads to a relative expansion of labor by these same firms. The gaps inMRPLwithin a region will not change in this case because labor will be reallocated across firms to keep these gaps constant. In contrast, labor misallocation effects can be important if the composition of credit affects labor distortions. The key pattern that allows one to disentangle these two cases is theMRPLgap between more and less productive firms. This gap will only be reduced in the case in which labor is not a sideshow.
We next examine whether our previous results on the effects of state banking deregulation are associated with convergence in the marginal revenue products of local firms with different productivity. This is the central qualitative prediction of the above framework. One challenge in the implementation of this analysis is that banking deregulation may affect the dispersion of marginal products through mechanisms other than changes in the composition of credit supply. As in the previous empirical analysis, we address this issue by comparing results for young versus old firms. We estimate specifications from our financial policy analyses (TableVI) using simple measures of gaps in firms’ marginal revenue products as the outcome variables. As in our financial policy test, this analysis focuses on longer-term changes in outcomes across Census years given that gaps in marginal revenue products can only be measured for broad samples of small firms during these years. We focus on the specifications that include an interaction withYoung. We therefore rely on this age interaction to control for demand effects.33
We measure gaps in firms’ marginal revenue products of labor usinglog(MRPL)=log(RevL), whereRevis firm revenue. Note that variation in this variable within an industry-state-age group-year captures gaps in firms’ marginal revenue product of labor (in logs) if firms’ labor elasticity is constant within these groups. This will be the case if firms have Cobb-Douglas revenue functions with parameters that depend only on these groups, a specification that is more flexible than that in the previous model.34We measure gaps in firms’ marginal revenue products of capital similarly. As in our previous results, we report scaled coefficients to capture differences between firms in the top and bottom quartiles of firm productivity.
TableIXreports the results. Column (1) reports results using the specification in Panel B of TableVI. We find that banking deregulation is associated with a significant reduction in the relative labor marginal product of high-productivity firms in the group of young firms. This effect is economically significant, implying that deregulation (a change inDeregfrom zero to two) is associated with a 5% decrease in the relative marginal product of these firms. This decrease represents a convergence of marginal products because, prior to deregulation, there is a significant positive link within an industry-state-year between the marginal product of labor and productivity of young firms in our sample. The estimated convergence effect represents a 12% decrease in the magnitude of this initial link. The results show that this convergence in the marginal product of labor holds across specifications. Column (2) considers the interaction of the specification in Panel C of TableVIwith age. All independent variables in this last specification are interacted withYoung. The coefficient of interestDereg × TFP × Young, estimates the differential value of the coefficient onDereg × TFPfor young firms. Here, we use a sample that is significantly larger than that used in TableVIand we are able to include interactions of both state fixed effects and year fixed effects withTFPas controls. Therefore, identification of the effect of interest is similar to that in column (1) and is based only on the differential changes for young firms in the link betweenMRPLandTFParound deregulation events. Compared to the previous result, the estimated effect following this approach is economically stronger.
Columns (3) to (5) show that these results remain important when we define young firms more broadly as firms with age between 1 and 10 years old. In the analysis that follows, estimates are based on the broader group of affected firms in quantifying the implications for aggregate industry productivity. Finally, column (6) shows that these estimates are associated with an economically similar effect for capital. As in the context of labor, we confirm that this effect captures convergence in the relative marginal products of firms with different productivity. Overall, this analysis suggests that the previous results lead to industry productivity gains and that labor misallocation effects plays a significant role in driving such gains.
In this section, we analyze the magnitude of the industry productivity gains implied by our previous results. Quantification of these implications requires additional assumptions about specific parameters and functional forms in the model. We interpret this analysis as describing what a standard model of misallocation used in the literature, together with reasonable parameter values, would imply for our results. Below, we describe our main findings; we provide greater detail in AppendixC. We assume that the credit composition effect analyzed in Section IV.B occurs for all states simultaneously and consider the gain for industry aggregate output. This gain is the sum of the reductions in local misallocation losses across regions. We evaluate this gain using equation8together with the previous expressions forσLgt2,σKgt2, andσKLgt. We use the values forβj,αj, andσAgt2implied by our estimates of firm productivity and follow the literature in setting σ between three and four. We use the estimates for changes inaLgandaKgfrom TableIX(columns (3) and (6)), which capture our broadest group of affected firms. We estimate the initial link between firms’ marginal revenue products andTFPprior to deregulation and use these estimates as initial values foraLgandaKg. This allows us to consider values of these parameters before and after deregulation. This is the only source of change in our analysis and is intended to capture the previous shift in the composition of credit. Additionally, we need to measure the share of firms in the different groups. Since we are adding effects across all regions, in this analysis we use the average share of young single-plant firms across industries. In the context of this analysis, we define young firms as firms that are 10 years old or less. Of the firms in our data, 56% are young single-plant firms.
TableXreports the results from this analysis. We report output gains in terms of value-added because it avoids double counting output across industries.35The results imply gains in aggregate industry value-added between 1.1% and 2.1%. These magnitudes are not sensitive to the choice of σ and are between 2.0% and 2.1% if we focus on industries in the top tercile of productivity dispersion. These effects capture gains over a period of approximately five years. The results also imply a reduction in local misallocation losses of 24%. Additionally, we consider the percentage increase in the gross output of young single-plant firms necessary to generate the same increase in industry value-added. One can interpret such increases as the equivalent gains in the (revenue) firm-level productivity of these firms necessary to generate the above effects. We find that these equivalent increases are economically large, representing implied increases in firm productivity between 3.3% and 6.2%.
Finally, we consider the role of labor in driving these implied gains. We follow a simple approach to decompose the estimates above into capital and labor misallocation effects. We first compute the gains assuming that onlyaKgdrops and there is no labor misallocation effect. We then estimate the incremental gains associated with the decrease inaLgat the new lower value foraKg, which gives us the contribution of reductions in labor misallocation, and we compute the percentage of the total gains associated with each of these components. We find that 85% to 86% of the gains come from the decrease in labor misallocation. Since the estimated decreases inaKgandaLgare similar, this gap comes from the difference in factor shares. Our analysis suggests that the previous credit effects lead to comparable changes in the costs of using capital and labor but that the labor effect is quantitatively more important because the labor share in production is significantly larger.
article.VI. Conclusions:
We study how state banking deregulation in the United States shapes the allocation of resources across firms with different productivity within local nonfinancial industries. We find that the deregulation of local U.S. banking markets leads to significant increases in the relative employment and capital growth of young, high-productivity firms. Using financial data for a broad range of firms, we further find that a shift in the composition of bank credit supply toward these young, high-productivity firms accompanies the real resource reallocations. We provide evidence that alternative mechanisms such as changes to local demand and other local economic conditions do not explain our growth results.
Intuitively, this reallocation effect due to bank lending should translate into gains in aggregate industry productivity. Building on a standard monopolistic competition framework used in the misallocation literature, we explicitly consider the implications of this effect for aggregate industry productivity. We then use this framework to quantify the implications of our results for aggregate industry productivity. We find that our results imply significant gains in aggregate industry productivity. In contrast to typical models connecting finance to aggregate productivity, we find that the reallocation of labor plays an important role in explaining these gains. We highlight how this role of labor should be important if the availability of credit affects firms’ cost of using labor.
Overall, our analysis suggests that increased competition in local banking markets increases credit to young, highly productive firms and can impact aggregate industry productivity. More broadly, our emphasis on the composition of bank lending and the allocation of labor complements previous evidence on the real effects of other reforms to financial or banking markets. Our results highlight the importance of incorporating bank competition and the allocation of labor when modeling the potential implications of finance for industry productivity.

article.Appendix A: Variable Definitions:
As described in SectionII, our main data sources are the Longitudinal Business Database (LBD), the Census of Manufacturers (CM), and the Quarterly Financial Reports (QFR) of the U.S. Census Bureau. Across all variables, industries are defined at the three-digit SIC code level.
Employment—total firm employment from the LBD. Given our sample of single-plant firms, this is the same as total establishment employment.
Employment Growth—change in the log of firm employment between yearstandt−1.
Dereg—banking deregulation index equal to the sum ofIntrastate_DeregandInterstate_Dereg, which are indicators that equal one if the state has passed intrastate and interstate banking deregulation, respectively.
Sales—total value of shipments from the CM adjusted by the industry deflator. This value is based on the last CM.
Young1, Young2, andOld—Young1is the group of firms present for the first time in the previous CM.Young2is the group of firms present for the first time in the CM prior to the previous one.Oldincludes all firms present in a prior CM that are not included in the two previous groups.
Age1andAge2—Age1is the firm's age measured using the LBD, that is, the number of years since the firm's first appearance in the LBD.Age2is the squared value ofAge1.
Large—indicator variable equal to one if the firm is above the median employment in a given year and age group (Young1, Young2, and other firms).
TFP—log of firm total factor productivity. We specify Cobb-Douglas production functions at the plant level with parameters that depend on an industry-year. Firm productivity is the same as plant productivity in our sample of single-plant firms. Output in the production function is industry-deflated sales. The inputs are the capital stock, labor (total hours), and total material costs. Production function parameters for an industry-year are estimated using plant-level data from the Annual Survey of Manufacturers (ASM) for the last five years. The ASM provides annual plant-level data for a random subsample of plants.TFPis measured in the last CM using values for the output and the inputs in that CM combined with the estimated production function parameters for that industry-year. In the Internet Appendix we consider alternative measures of firmTFPbased on different approaches to estimating the production function parameters.36
Capital Stock Growth—change in the firms’ capital stock between the end of yearstandt−1, from the CM.
Young (Age 1–10), Young (Age 1–5), Young (Age 6–10), andOld (11+)—age groups constructed during Census years based on the number of years since the firm's first appearance in the CM.
Bank Debt Share—ratio of bank debt (from commercial banks) to the sum of bank debt and nonbank debt from the QFR (balance sheet information).
Bank Debt Ratio—ratio of bank debt (from commercial banks) to the book value of total assets from the QFR (balance sheet information).
Nonbank Debt Ratio—ratio of nonbank debt (from sources excluding commercial banks) to the book value of total assets from the QFR (balance sheet information).
IDereg—value ofDeregat the start of the sample (1977).
Pre—indicator equal to one prior to the deregulation wave (in 1977 and 1982). This variable is used in specifications estimated only with Census years.
Shock(TableVII)—In Panel A,Shockis a predicted shock to state economic conditions between 1977 and 1993 (sample period). For each state, we first determine the initial share (in 1977) of the total employment in each industry (initial share). We then estimate the national-level employment growth of each industry in this list using data for other states (national industry growth). The predicted growth rate is the weighted average of these national growth rates across industries, where the previous initial shares are used as weights. In Panel B,Shockis the state's aggregate employment growth between 1977 and 1993 (sample period). These employment variables are based on LBD employment data.
Shock(TableVIII)—In Panel A,Shockis a predicted shock to state economic conditions between 1977 (first sample year) and the current year, constructed in an analogous way as in Panel A of TableVII. Initial shares are based on 1977 and the national-level growth rate of each industry is defined between 1977 and the current year. In Panel B,Shockis the state's aggregate employment growth between 1977 and the current year. In Panels C to E,Shockis predicted in an analogous way as in Panel A, but includes only a subset of local industries for each industry-state. More specifically, for a given industry, we first define connected industries at the national level (see below). When predicting a shock for a given industry-state, we only include connected industries in the same state. The predicted growth for connected industries is the weighted average of the national growth rates across industries, defined as in Panel A, but now restricted to this subset of connected industries. This predicted growth rate is multiplied by the initial share of connected industries in the state to better capture the magnitude of the shock. We follow Ellison, Glaeser, and Kerr (2010) in defining connections. In Panels C and D, connected industries are defined using the Benchmark Input-Output Accounts published by the Bureau of Economic Analysis (BEA). Panel C classifies industryjas connected (buyer) to industryiif it purchases at least 5% of the total sales from industryi. Panel D classifies industryjas connected (seller) to industryiif at least 5% of the total inputs used by industryicomes from industryj. In Panel E, connected industries are defined using the National Industrial-Occupation Employment Matrix (NIOEM) published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS). The NIOEM provides industry-level employment in 277 occupations. We first estimate the share of each industry across occupations. We then assess the degree of similarity of the labor used by two industries by measuring the correlation of their shares across different occupations. Industries are classified as connected if their correlation is in the top 25% of the distribution across all industry pairs.

article.Appendix B: Analysis of Model of Misallo:
We show how the model in Section VI.A leads to the expression for local misallocation losses(LMLjgt) in the text (equation8). We also provide more economic intuition behind this result.
We start by describing in detail the intuition behind a key point in this framework, namely, gaps in firms’ marginal revenue products translate into lower aggregate industry productivity (misallocation losses).
Intuitively, industry productivity can be increased by reallocating factorFfrom firmito firmkonly ifpkt∂Qkt∂F>pit∂Qit∂F. In other words, we can evaluate the contribution of products to industry productivity using their prices. This follows from the fact that the marginal rate of transformation (MRT) between two products in the industry production function (representative firm) is given by their relative price, that is,MRTjti,i′=PijtPi′jt.
This intuitive result comes directly from the first-order condition for the problem of the representative firm:maxQijt{PjtQjt−∑iPijtQijt}, whereQjt=(∑iQijtσ−1σ)σσ−1and the representative firm is buying output from firms in the industry. This problem leads to demand for differentiated products with a constant price elasticityσas given by(QijtQjt)=(PijtPjt)−σ. The revenue function of the firm is given byRijt=PijtQijt. From this, the marginal revenue products of any given factor can be written as∂Rijt∂Fijt=Pijt∂Qijt∂Fijt+∂Pijt∂FijtQijt. Therefore, under this framework, resource reallocation across firms can translate into industry productivity gains if the marginal revenue products of these firms are not equalized. article.Appendix C: Assumptions for Quantificati:
We provide a more detailed description of the assumptions that we use for the different parameters in the quantification exercise. The values for α and β are based on the estimatesα∼andβ∼in ourTFPestimation. From our analysis in AppendixB, we can obtainαand β asα=(σσ−1)α∼andβ=(σσ−1)β∼. When choosing values forσ, we use three following Klenow and Hsieh (2009) and four as in Bloom (2009). We estimateSharegdirectly from the data as the average share of young single-plant firms across industries, which is 56% in our sample. Notice that we are adding gains across all local industries, so we use the total share of this group in an industry. We measureaLgempirically by first estimating a linear regression ofMRPLonTFP(in logs) using single-establishment young firms (age 1–10) in 1977, where we include only those states that have not been deregulated in the given year. We include industry-state-age group fixed effects in this regression. This regression allows us to predictlog(1+τL)withTFP(in logs) within a group prior to deregulation. This in turn provides an initial value foraLg, which we combine with the estimated effect from TableIXto obtain a final value foraLg. We follow an analogous procedure to analyzeaKg. To obtain an empirical proxy forσAg2, for each industry (three-digit SIC code), state, and year, we first compute the standard deviation ofTFP(in logs) among young (age 1–10) single-plant firms. We then take the average across states and years to obtain a measure for each industry. Next, we then sort all industries based on this measure. In our empirical analyses, we use the average value across all industries, as well as the average values for the top 50% and top 33% of industries.
Using the initial and final values for(aLg,aKg)and the other values previously discussed, we can computeΔLML. To convert these gross output gains into value-added gains, we scaleΔLML(in gross output) by the average ratio of industry sales to industry value-added (see SectionVI). We convert this gain into an equivalent change in the output of young single-plant firms by dividingΔLMLby the average share of output among young single-plant firms across industries.
